topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the locker room was a physical weight pressing down on Elias. Every scrape of a shoe, every rustle of fabric, seemed amplified in the cavernous space. He kept running a hand over the smooth, cold metal of his water bottle, his knuckles whitening slightly. The air felt too thick, the fluorescent lights buzzing with an irritating, high-pitched whine that grated against his nerves. He stared at the roster taped to the wall, his eyes tracing the names, landing repeatedly on the empty space where his name should have been. A tremor started in his left hand, making him grip the edge of the bench so tightly his fingers ached. "Right," he managed, his voice coming out a little too tight, forcing a laugh that sounded brittle even to his own ears. He pushed himself up, the movement stiff, and walked toward the door, every step measured and deliberate, trying to keep the frantic thrumming beneath his ribs from becoming visible.

[story 2]

I kept checking my phone, though I knew the notifications wouldn't change anything. The waiting room smelled faintly of industrial cleaner and stale coffee, a scent that usually grounded me, but today it just made my stomach clench. My jaw was locked so tight I could feel the strain in my temples. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, a restless, almost involuntary movement. Every time a door clicked shut down the hall, I flinched, my shoulders hunching instinctively toward my ears. *This isn't happening,* I thought, a cold knot tightening in my chest. The receptionist offered a practiced, overly bright smile, and I managed a nod, my gaze fixed somewhere beyond her shoulder, unwilling to meet anyone’s eyes.

[story 3]

The coach’s words echoed in the empty training facility, hollow and sharp. Liam paced the perimeter of the track, his breathing shallow and rapid, his gaze darting from the starting blocks to the distant bleachers. He kept flexing his jaw, a habit he hadn't managed to break, the muscles in his neck corded with tension. He picked at a loose thread on his sweatpants, his movements jerky, inefficient. When the coach finally spoke, his voice was calm, almost disappointingly so, which only made Liam feel more unsettled. He swallowed hard, the dryness in his throat making the action difficult, and forced himself to meet the coach’s eyes, trying to project an assurance he absolutely did not feel.
